text: Welcome to another episode of This Is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Matt from
Benevity. Hey Matt. Hey, how's it going? Tell us a little bit about what Benevity does.
Yeah, Benevity is trusted by the world's most purpose-driven brands, including Amazon,
to engage their employees and communities by offering donation, volunteering, and granting
programs. So today we're going to talk about all things logging, and you've had some experience
with that. Tell us about some of the challenges you've had. Absolutely. So in our legacy system,
we were spending a lot of time on patching and maintenance. So we were spending up to 16 hours
per month on those activities, which weren't a differentiating factor for us. We also had logs
moving into different systems. Our legacy EC2 logs were flowing into Logstash, but our Lambda and ECS
logs were often ending up in CloudWatch, and we needed something more centralized. Gotcha. I remember
building ElkStacks myself, so looking forward to understanding how you simplified this. So let's
dive in. Absolutely. So we had three layers of compute. As I said, EC2 was already moving through
Logstash, and we were able to port that directly over. For our Lambda layers, we were moving into the
Lambda that is provided in the OpenSearch documentation for log processing. And for ECS, we were using the
FluentBit container. So we're using FireLens to push logs to FluentBit, which is directly in the
task definition for our ECS tasks. And that would push the logs through to our centralized logging.
So you're using two different products to do log processing. How does that simplify things?
Yeah, we wanted the happiest and easiest path in both of these situations. So the Logstash that was
already set up for our EC2 instances, we were able to keep, which was a huge win for us. And being able to
put the FluentBit container as a sidecard directly in the ECS task definition made it really easy to
process those logs. Wow. So that really simplifies the packaging and deployment of that workload.
Absolutely. Great. Okay. So now I see you've got a bunch of users that I'm sure want to access all
this. How do they do that? Yeah. So all of the logs are coming from the processors into OpenSearch. So
now we have one centralized location where we can manage all of these logs. At the same time,
we have hundreds of users that want access from a number of different departments, including SecOps and
IT, our developers and engineers, and our operations folks. So we used Okta as a middle layer to provide
our back for our users going into OpenSearch. And that allows us to filter on different indexes for
different users so that we can have sensitive logging right beside regular application logging and provide
those logs to the users that need them. That's pretty clever. It's giving you a permission system,
on those indexes, right? Absolutely. Fantastic. What are some of the benefits that you've seen?
The big benefit for us from an operation standpoint is the removal of the 16 hours per month. We were up
against challenges as well with upgrading the versions of our legacy logging software. And we don't have to
have those problems with OpenSearch because it's really easy to move from one version to another.
We also now have a fully centralized solution so we can send all of our users to the same place,
which really helps us with getting the logs to the users that need them. Very clever. Thanks so much
for sharing your architecture with me today. Thank you. And thank you for joining us on This Is My Architecture.
